May, 1996

Rogainer Almost Does Last Relocation

Bob Reddick

On April 20th, 1996, while spectating at the Navy Recreation Center 24-hour Eco-Challenge (hike-bike-canoe) event at Jim Creek, WA, I was walking down a forested spur by myself and suddenly encountered an acute incipient large anteroseptal infarcation, later accompanied by five nasty little fibrillations – all of whom were extremely unfriendly!!! Let me assure you that I would much rather have met an angry Sasquatch face to face.

Just had time to dig out my orienteering whistle from my pack and signal for help. Thanks to God and a superbly organized and capable event support team, plus competitive team #5, within a very short time I was on a 14-footed litter going downhill to meet the just-arriving medevac van. Only took some clotbuster and five jumpstarts to get the old ticker going again, and then . . .

But enough of all that for now. On April 24th the experts diamond drilled me out, and I'm heading for home today. A lessons learned article is now in the works, for early publication in the IRB, Bearing 315, and O/NA (yes, you're now warned).

St. Edward '96 Trail Project

For the past four seasons, COC has hosted a trail building project at St. Edward State Park, site of our annual December orienteering event. Those of us who have pitched in have enjoyed our time helping make the park trail system grow. Moreover, in today's political climate, the orienteering community needs to not only be seen as "environmentally considerate" but as a group that is actually enhancing our park system. So, if you want to make a truly meaningful contribution and help spread the word that orienteers are helping with the trails system, please join us at one of the sponsored trail work parties. This year we are teaming up with the Backcountry Bicycle Trails Club and REI, so you can help with the project in June at any of the events. (Just call Rick Hood at 367-2606 or check the Hotline for specific dates.)

1996 SW Washington Invitational O Meet

Sunday, 14 July, starts 11am-1pm

Attention Washington State orienteers! If you're not making the long trek to the Colorado multi-day, come down to the SW corner of our state for summer orienteering at its best! BattleGround Lake SP offers a shaded picnic area, snack bar, playground, bathhouse with coin-operated showers and both drive-in and walk-in campsites. The woods are cool and beautiful and best of all, the deep crater lake offers the best swimming in the entire region!

Using a 1:10,000 OCAD map, CROC will offer White, Yellow, Orange, and a Norwegian "map memory" course where only small snips of the map showing the next leg will be posted at each control. Join us for an informal potluck picnic afterwards -- CROC will supply the hot dogs!

Page 2

Field Notes

  • The "O-Van 2" will be leaving Victoria on August 4 for Quebec and New York. There is room for one or two, for one for both ways. If you're interest, contact Carl Coger (work/fax: 604-380-6562, home: 604-592-4944).

  • The COC board again recognized that dedicated volunteers are what make the club and orienteering happen, and as such elected to more formalize the award program—"Presidential Service Award" for extensive service in the past few years and "Board of Directors' Life Service Award" for sustained and significant service for an extended number of years—that we started in 1994. (In the next issue we will explain the programs.)

  • You probably felt the recent magnitude 5.3 earthquake in the area. Current data shows the epicenter to be a few miles east of Duvall, directly under our Drunken Charlie O-Map!

  • The COC wished to thank Ed Ledger, who has been heading up the club's publicity efforts this past year. Unfortunately for us, Ed has stepped down and thus the chair is open. If you are interested, please give Margie a call at 634-2279.

  • Eric Bone repeated as the U.S. Intercollegiate Varsity Individual Champion for the fourth consecutive year, becoming the first person ever to accomplish that feat. The UW Huskies team however managed only third place behind a much improved West Point team and a surprise second place team from Cornell University. Nicholas Bone finished 9th in the varsity men's category out of 39 competitors while Lisa Nordlund finished 5th of 9 in the varsity women's category.

  • Congratulations to COC's Eric Bone, who, along with BAOC's James Scarborough, has been selected to attend a world class training clinic at what many consider to be the premier orienteering event, the O-Ringen. Best wishes Eric!

  • Local orienteers have recently been becoming TV stars as well. Eileen Breseman was the only orienteer selected for a news piece on a Arizona station at the two-day "A" meet in February; Bill Cusworth similarly made an appearance on a mid-west station, and Mike Schuh was again seen orienteering on the tube when PBS repeated a "Bill Nye the Science Guy" episode.

  • On Saturday June 1 at Camp Koinonia after the grand dinner COC will be hosting a series of short informal entertainment, starting around 7pm, including Micro "O" 2.0 and Map ID "O" — the hard version. (Anyone that wishes to share something extending beyond 10 minutes should check with Rick at 367-2606; thanks.)

  • The 25th Northwest Folklife Festival will be held Memorial Day weekend at the Seattle Center. The free event features music and dancing (and yes, Eric, food) from Friday evening, May 24th, through Monday night—three and a half days. So what does this have to do with orienteering? Little, beyond using a map of the center grounds to find your way from one of the 20 stages to the next—and the fact that Mike Schuh will be calling a contra dance at 6:00 PM Friday evening in the "Roadhouse" (Flag Pavilion West). Remember: it's free, all dances are taught (walked through) prior to dancing them, and—most of all—it's fun. See you there!

  • The Jefferson State O Club, of Medford Oregon, has decided to suspend operations. They have withdrawn from USOF, are not putting on any 1996 O events, and are returning their USOF Club Starter Kit. To quote from Terry Miller, one of the chief organizers: "The end result we envisioned was a small but strong participating group that held a potential for growth. We have seen nothing of the sort happening. It is very disheartening to spend the time and effort required to stage an event and find ourselves alone or instructing one or two people new to O, and so able to run the most elemental course, or interested in the smaller course only as an introduction to what this sport is. Many times the second (more advanced) course we set had no one on it."

  • On a trial basis, COC will offer pre-marked maps for some local events on a first come basis. (Bill Cusworth has generously offered to make color originals with courses pre-printed for the maps we have on OCAD at about ½ what it would cost to do at a copy center.) So if you want the best chance of getting a pre-marked map at a local event, get there closer to 10am then noon. Of course, a lot of folks enjoy marking their own map and you will always have that option.

RETURN TO THE RIDGE

Cle Elum on June 1st and 2nd

is the place to be for two great days of orienteering

For those new to the "Cle Elum Ridge" map, the terrain is open or lightly forested with rolling hills making for fast times. (The 1:15,000 IOF quality map was updated in 1993.)

On Saturday, for the one-day stand-alone meet directed by Debbie Newell, and with courses designed by Nic Bone, we are offering 6 nice options, with an open class structure:

Number/Name Length
1. Novice 1.5 - 2.0 k
2. Beginner 2.0 - 2.5 k
3. Intermediate 4.0 - 4.5 k
4. Short Advanced 4.5 - 5.5 k
5. Medium Advanced 7.0 - 8.0 k
6. Long Advanced 11.0 - 13.0 k

There will be at least one water control on courses 3, 4, 5, and 6 as well as water at registration and the start/finish area. We, however, encourage you to bring supplemental water, especially if the day is hot.

Starts will be from 11am to 1pm. Those who register by mail in time will receive a pre-marked map and pre-assigned start time. (Those not registering in time will have to mark their own map and wait for a start time opening.)

Winners will be recognized at the dinner at Camp Koinonia, where camping, showers, and breakfast are also an option if registered. (Of course everyone is most welcome to join us for the after-dinner informal entertainment and socializing, starting at 7pm.)

Page 4

On Sunday look for the first ever "Pig War Relay". This will be the only Pig War event in 1996, so while it looks like the US has a pretty big home map advantage, the Canadians have warned us "not to count them out" and we should believe them!!! (Next year the Pig War will likely also be a single one-day relay event, but in British Columbia.)

Peter Golde and Rick Hood, Sunday's course setter and meet director respectively, in consultation with OABC's Carl Coger, artist and "keeper of the pig trophy" have come up with the following relay format for your enjoyment and challenge.

Three-person teams with each of the three legs having a unique flavor (i.e., one leg might be listed as: more technical with more climb but shorter). You pick and register your own team on Saturday before 7:30pm for either of two categories based on an "age, gender, and experience" point system. Relay awards are based solely on teams' elapsed time in their category. (For example, the "Gold" Category requires 2-4 points while the "Blue" one requires 5 or more points.) Details on course legs, point system, mass start times, and Pig War scoring will be posted at the meet on Saturday. (Saturday's dinner is a great chance to pick someone to "run around with" or "sell your services"; who knows what you're worth!)

Please note, there will be no day of event registration on Sunday, nor will there be a non-relay option. (Ellensburg OC's Will Sperry, however, will have an informal beginner course for those interested.)

We hope you join us for either or both of these two great events at Cle Elum this June 1st and 2nd, as well as join us for dinner Saturday night with informal fun activities afterwards.

To reach the event parking for either event from Seattle, take Interstate 90 east to exit number 85 and follow the O road signs. From the parking/registration area there is about a 10 -15 minute walk to the start/finish, where Ellensburg OC will again have their famous cookies and soft drink stand. (We will also have the not-so-famous COC port-o-potty, so you might want to plan ahead.)

For more information please feel free to call Debbie Newell at 206 488-7471 or Rick Hood at 206 367-2606. or 71753.2332@compuserve.com

Hope to see you at Cle Elum Ridge this June 1st and 2nd!

Blue Moon Rogaine

SAGE ROGAINE VIII
BRITISH COLUMBIA
ROGAINE CHAMPIONSHIPS
JUNE 22-23, 1996

WHAT IS ROGAINE?
The ultimate test of body, mind and soul. For the serious competitor, it's an ultra-marathon with an atitude. For the casual competitor it's a rewarding walk in the woods. The term ROGAINE is an acronym derived from Rugged Outdoor Group Activity Involving Navigation and Endurance. A ROGAINE is a little bit like a treasure hunt. Teams of 2 to 5 persons try to find "controls" distributed over a 100 to 200 sq km area using a map and compass. Each control (which usually consists of an orange and white flag with a special punch attached) is worth a certain number of points. The goal is to accumulate as many points as possible over a specified time period (usually 24 hours). The elite will cover 80-100km with 3-4000m of climbing in a competitive test of endurance and navigation, for others, it can simply be a leisurely hike through the woods. If you like the outdoors and want a little, or a lot of adventure, give rogaining a try!

LOCATION: Located in the Douglas Fir and Ponderosa Pine forests of the Thompson Plateau near Savona B.C..

START: Noon, June 22, 1996. Maps will be handed out at 09:30 A.M.

FEATURES:

  • Classic 24 hour and 12 hour events
  • 2 to 5 person teams allowed.
  • Prizes in all events
  • Great food and drinks provided at the Hash House (event center) from 4:00pm Saturday through to a hot lunch and awards ceremony at 1:00pm Sunday.
  • Dinner, breakfast and lunch are included in event fee!
  • Open, masters, and recreational categories
  • Pre-marked 1:50,000 scale maps for all competitors
  • Assistance for first time rogainers

CONFIRMATION: A detailed information package will be sent to team contacts upon receipt of application and event fee. This package will contain event rules, directions to event camp, equipment requirements, etc.

Where and what are these Orienteering Clubs?

The Orienteering clubs listed here are independently administered non-profit organizations chartered by the United Stated Orienteering Federation. Sammamish primarily operates in the east-side (Bellevue and Redmond) area, while Cascade (the largest club) operates in the greater Seattle area. Ellensburg is centered in that town, Sacajawea is based in the Tri-Cities area, and the new Chuckanut O.C. is based in Bellingham. Husky Orienteering is for University of Washington students, faculty, and staff. Cascade and Sammamish clubs hold orienteering events throughout the year in the Puget Sound area, and eastward into the Cascade Mountains; the other clubs hold events in their areas. Membership is open to everyone, and entitles you to event discounts, club voting rights, and includes a subscription to Bearing 315, published six times per year.

What equipment do I need?

The only requirement is a compass. The best kind is a protractor-type with a clear plastic base so that you can see through it to the map underneath. Compasses are available for loan (with deposit) at orienteering events if you don't own one. At each event you will receive a detailed topographic map of the area, usually produced specifically for orienteering by the club. Long pants (light nylon is best, but jeans will do) and long-sleeve shirts are recommended because of sometimes dense underbrush. Jogging shoes or lightweight hiking boots are preferable; your feet may get wet and muddy, so a spare pair socks and shoes are nice. Bring a watch so you can keep track of the time.

The weather for the Saturday meet was fantastic, as was the view from Little Summit where the registration and finish were located. Attendance was light as was expected but there were a few recreational orienteers, incuding 2 first-timers, in attendance. Most of the advanced orienteers opted for Course 4, the longer of the two advanced courses.

Most of the comments about the courses were positive except one control on course 4 (which was in an area that was not quite mapped correctly) and the 11 contour (55 m) climb from the last control to the finish. Comet viewing was very popular Saturday night due to the clear skies and no city lights. An arctic express blew in overnight bringing very strong wind gusts and freezing temperatures which continued Sunday morning for the training exercises. Six people braved the cold and did at least some of the exercises planned. I hope we will be able to hold an event at Moran SP next year.

What is USOF?

Often in this newsletter, or in orienteering conversation, you will hear references to "yousoff," and you may have seen the magazine "Orienteering/North America." But why do they need so much money, you ask? We charge $10/year for local dues, so why should it cost an extra $25 to join an organization that doesn't seem to do much? The answer, of course, is that USOF does quite a bit.

The largest part of USOF dues goes for the O/NA subscription. This is not only a great source of information, it is well written and edited, and has many articles of general interest on health, exercise, and training. From time to time, we reprint articles from O/NA, and there are always copies available at major meets and club meetings. Most of the rest of USOF dues goes to pay for insurance. Although we, as individuals, might be willing to waive insurance, virtually all land owners, both public and private, require insurance as a precondition even to talking about using their land for orienteering. USOF members get a small rebate, about $2 per national meet, to help balance out the insurance, but, basically, USOF subsidizes our meets. Without USOF, I doubt that either Cascade or Sammamish could afford the insurance to hold another meet.

So we encourage you not only to join your local club, but to join and support USOF. The magazine along is worth the cost. You will also find as many opportunities to serve on national committees as there are to serve locally. USOF, like our local clubs, has no paid employees, and does not pay travel or per diem expenses for its officers. USOF, like Cascade, is an IRS section 501c(3) corporation. Which means that donations, including costs, are fully tax deductible. JOIN NOW!

block_index.text: BEARING 315 [Logo: Runner silhouette] [Logo: Cascade Orienteering compass] The Newsletter of Orienteering in the Pacific Northwest May, 1996

On April 20th, 1996, while spectating at the Navy Recreation Center 24-hour Eco-Challenge (hike-bike-canoe) event at Jim Creek, WA, I was walking down a forested spur by myself and suddenly encountered an acute incipient large anteroseptal infarcation, later accompanied by five nasty little fibrillations – all of whom were extremely unfriendly!!! Let me assure you that I would much rather have met an angry Sasquatch face to face.

Just had time to dig out my orienteering whistle from my pack and signal for help. Thanks to God and a superbly organized and capable event support team, plus competitive team #5, within a very short time I was on a 14-footed litter going downhill to meet the just-arriving medevac van. Only took some clotbuster and five jumpstarts to get the old ticker going again, and then . . .

But enough of all that for now. On April 24th the experts diamond drilled me out, and I'm heading for home today. A lessons learned article is now in the works, for early publication in the IRB, Bearing 315, and O/NA (yes, you're now warned).

Copyright © 1996, Cascade Orienteering Club. Permission is given to reproduce for free distribution for the promotion of orienteering. Bearing 315 is published six times each year by Cascade Orienteering Club.

For the past four seasons, COC has hosted a trail building project at St. Edward State Park, site of our annual December orienteering event. Those of us who have pitched in have enjoyed our time helping make the park trail system grow. Moreover, in today's political climate, the orienteering community needs to not only be seen as "environmentally considerate" but as a group that is actually enhancing our park system. So, if you want to make a truly meaningful contribution and help spread the word that orienteers are helping with the trails system, please join us at one of the sponsored trail work parties. This year we are teaming up with the Backcountry Bicycle Trails Club and REI, so you can help with the project in June at any of the events. (Just call Rick Hood at 367-2606 or check the Hotline for specific dates.)

Attention Washington State orienteers! If you're not making the long trek to the Colorado multi-day, come down to the SW corner of our state for summer orienteering at its best! BattleGround Lake SP offers a shaded picnic area, snack bar, playground, bathhouse with coin-operated showers and both drive-in and walk-in campsites. The woods are cool and beautiful and best of all, the deep crater lake offers the best swimming in the entire region!

Using a 1:10,000 OCAD map, CROC will offer White, Yellow, Orange, and a Norwegian "map memory" course where only small snips of the map showing the next leg will be posted at each control. Join us for an informal potluck picnic afterwards -- CROC will supply the hot dogs!

The "O-Van 2" will be leaving Victoria on August 4 for Quebec and New York. There is room for one or two, for one for both ways. If you're interest, contact Carl Coger (work/fax: 604-380-6562, home: 604-592-4944).

The COC board again recognized that dedicated volunteers are what make the club and orienteering happen, and as such elected to more formalize the award program—"Presidential Service Award" for extensive service in the past few years and "Board of Directors' Life Service Award" for sustained and significant service for an extended number of years—that we started in 1994. (In the next issue we will explain the programs.)
You probably felt the recent magnitude 5.3 earthquake in the area. Current data shows the epicenter to be a few miles east of Duvall, directly under our Drunken Charlie O-Map!

The COC wished to thank Ed Ledger, who has been heading up the club's publicity efforts this past year. Unfortunately for us, Ed has stepped down and thus the chair is open. If you are interested, please give Margie a call at 634-2279.

Eric Bone repeated as the U.S. Intercollegiate Varsity Individual Champion for the fourth consecutive year, becoming the first person ever to accomplish that feat. The UW Huskies team however managed only third place behind a much improved West Point team and a surprise second place team from Cornell University. Nicholas Bone finished 9th in the varsity men's category out of 39 competitors while Lisa Nordlund finished 5th of 9 in the varsity women's category.

Congratulations to COC's Eric Bone, who, along with BAOC's James Scarborough, has been selected to attend a world class training clinic at what many consider to be the premier orienteering event, the O-Ringen. Best wishes Eric!

Local orienteers have recently been becoming TV stars as well. Eileen Breseman was the only orienteer selected for a news piece on a Arizona station at the two-day "A" meet in February; Bill Cusworth similarly made an appearance on a mid-west station, and Mike Schuh was again seen orienteering on the tube when PBS repeated a "Bill Nye the Science Guy" episode.

On Saturday June 1 at Camp Koinonia after the grand dinner COC will be hosting a series of short informal entertainment, starting around 7pm, including Micro "O" 2.0 and Map ID "O" — the hard version. (Anyone that wishes to share something extending beyond 10 minutes should check with Rick at 367-2606; thanks.)

The 25th Northwest Folklife Festival will be held Memorial Day weekend at the Seattle Center. The free event features music and dancing (and yes, Eric, food) from Friday evening, May 24th, through Monday night—three and a half days. So what does this have to do with orienteering? Little, beyond using a map of the center grounds to find your way from one of the 20 stages to the next—and the fact that Mike Schuh will be calling a contra dance at 6:00 PM Friday evening in the "Roadhouse" (Flag Pavilion West). Remember: it's free, all dances are taught (walked through) prior to dancing them, and—most of all—it's fun. See you there!

The Jefferson State O Club, of Medford Oregon, has decided to suspend operations. They have withdrawn from USOF, are not putting on any 1996 O events, and are returning their USOF Club Starter Kit. To quote from Terry Miller, one of the chief organizers: "The end result we envisioned was a small but strong participating group that held a potential for growth. We have seen nothing of the sort happening. It is very disheartening to spend the time and effort required to stage an event and find ourselves alone or instructing one or two people new to O, and so able to run the most elemental course, or interested in the smaller course only as an introduction to what this sport is. Many times the second (more advanced) course we set had no one on it."

On a trial basis, COC will offer pre-marked maps for some local events on a first come basis. (Bill Cusworth has generously offered to make color originals with courses pre-printed for the maps we have on OCAD at about ½ what it would cost to do at a copy center.) So if you want the best chance of getting a pre-marked map at a local event, get there closer to 10am then noon. Of course, a lot of folks enjoy marking their own map and you will always have that option.

SAGE ROGAINE VIII BRITISH COLUMBIA ROGAINE CHAMPIONSHIPS JUNE 22-23, 1996

WHAT IS ROGAINE? The ultimate test of body, mind and soul. For the serious competitor, it's an ultra-marathon with an atitude. For the casual competitor it's a rewarding walk in the woods. The term ROGAINE is an acronym derived from Rugged Outdoor Group Activity Involving Navigation and Endurance. A ROGAINE is a little bit like a treasure hunt. Teams of 2 to 5 persons try to find "controls" distributed over a 100 to 200 sq km area using a map and compass. Each control (which usually consists of an orange and white flag with a special punch attached) is worth a certain number of points. The goal is to accumulate as many points as possible over a specified time period (usually 24 hours). The elite will cover 80-100km with 3-4000m of climbing in a competitive test of endurance and navigation, for others, it can simply be a leisurely hike through the woods. If you like the outdoors and want a little, or a lot of adventure, give rogaining a try!

Where and what are these Orienteering Clubs?

The Orienteering clubs listed here are independently administered non-profit organizations chartered by the United Stated Orienteering Federation. Sammamish primarily operates in the east-side (Bellevue and Redmond) area, while Cascade (the largest club) operates in the greater Seattle area. Ellensburg is centered in that town, Sacajawea is based in the Tri-Cities area, and the new Chuckanut O.C. is based in Bellingham. Husky Orienteering is for University of Washington students, faculty, and staff. Cascade and Sammamish clubs hold orienteering events throughout the year in the Puget Sound area, and eastward into the Cascade Mountains; the other clubs hold events in their areas. Membership is open to everyone, and entitles you to event discounts, club voting rights, and includes a subscription to Bearing 315, published six times per year.

The only requirement is a compass. The best kind is a protractor-type with a clear plastic base so that you can see through it to the map underneath. Compasses are available for loan (with deposit) at orienteering events if you don't own one. At each event you will receive a detailed topographic map of the area, usually produced specifically for orienteering by the club. Long pants (light nylon is best, but jeans will do) and long-sleeve shirts are recommended because of sometimes dense underbrush. Jogging shoes or lightweight hiking boots are preferable; your feet may get wet and muddy, so a spare pair socks and shoes are nice. Bring a watch so you can keep track of the time.

Often in this newsletter, or in orienteering conversation, you will hear references to "yousoff," and you may have seen the magazine "Orienteering/North America." But why do they need so much money, you ask? We charge $10/year for local dues, so why should it cost an extra $25 to join an organization that doesn't seem to do much? The answer, of course, is that USOF does quite a bit.

The largest part of USOF dues goes for the O/NA subscription. This is not only a great source of information, it is well written and edited, and has many articles of general interest on health, exercise, and training. From time to time, we reprint articles from O/NA, and there are always copies available at major meets and club meetings. Most of the rest of USOF dues goes to pay for insurance. Although we, as individuals, might be willing to waive insurance, virtually all land owners, both public and private, require insurance as a precondition even to talking about using their land for orienteering. USOF members get a small rebate, about $2 per national meet, to help balance out the insurance, but, basically, USOF subsidizes our meets. Without USOF, I doubt that either Cascade or Sammamish could afford the insurance to hold another meet.

So we encourage you not only to join your local club, but to join and support USOF. The magazine along is worth the cost. You will also find as many opportunities to serve on national committees as there are to serve locally. USOF, like our local clubs, has no paid employees, and does not pay travel or per diem expenses for its officers. USOF, like Cascade, is an IRS section 501c(3) corporation. Which means that donations, including costs, are fully tax deductible. JOIN NOW!
